plainMeaning: Bowing low, I take up the names of the living Jinas Sujāta, Svayamprabha, Ṛṣabhānana, Anantavīrya and Sūraprabha, and I offer them my worship.
recitation: sujāta svayaṃprabha svāmī, ṛṣabhānana lehuṁ nāmī / anaṃtavīraja deva, suraprabhu, karuṁ ya seva

wordByWord.gloss: Sujāta — a living (viharamāṇa) Jina; literally 'the well-born one'
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit su- ('good, well') + jāta ('born'); a proper name of the fifth of the twenty living Jinas

wordByWord.gloss: lord, master (an honorific attached to the Jina's name)
wordByWord.notes: Threaded between the names as a title, not a separate deity.

wordByWord.gloss: Ṛṣabhānana — a living Jina; 'bull-faced', bearing the likeness of Ṛṣabha
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ṛṣabha ('bull') + ānana ('face, mouth'); the same name also figures among the four śāśvata (eternal) Jina-names

wordByWord.gloss: I take up / I utter (the name)
wordByWord.etymology: Old/medieval Gujarati levuṁ ('to take') from Sanskrit root labh-; first-person singular
wordByWord.notes: Here 'take the name', i.e. invoke aloud.

wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati namvuṁ ('to bow') from Sanskrit root nam-; absolutive/participial sense
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with lehuṁ: 'bowing, I take the name.'

wordByWord.gloss: Anantavīrya — a living Jina; 'of infinite valour/energy'
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ananta ('endless, infinite') + vīrya ('valour, virile energy'); the verse adds a light metrical -ja to vīra

wordByWord.gloss: god, divine lord (honorific for the Jina)
Sūraprabha — a living Jina; here voiced as 'lord of the gods'
wordByWord.etymology: Standard name Sūraprabha = sūra ('sun') + prabhā ('splendour'); the verse vocalizes it as sura ('god') + prabhu ('lord'), a devotional pun
wordByWord.notes: Ninth of the twenty living Jinas.

wordByWord.gloss: I do, I render, I perform
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati karvuṁ from Sanskrit root kṛ-; first-person singular
Connective particle (cf. Prakrit ya / Sanskrit ca, 'and')
wordByWord.notes: Binds this verse's 'service' to the bowing just expressed: 'I too render service.'

wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit sevā ('attendance, service, devotion'), shortened metrically to sev

literalTranslation: Sujāta, Svayamprabha the lord, (and) Ṛṣabhānana — bowing, I take up (their names); Anantavīrya the god, (and) Sūraprabha the lord — I too render service.
idiomaticTranslation: To Sujāta and to lord Svayamprabha, and bowing low I take up the name of Ṛṣabhānana; to the divine Anantavīrya and to Sūraprabha, lord among the gods, I too offer my loving service.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This stanza continues the roll-call of the viharamāṇa (currently-living) Jinas opened in the previous verse with Sīmandhar, Jugmandhar, Bāhu and Subāhu. Here the poet names the next five of the twenty — Sujāta, Svayamprabha, Ṛṣabhānana, Anantavīrya and Sūraprabha — and the following verse carries the garland onward (Viśāla, Vajradhara, Candrānana, Candrabāhu…). What lifts it above a bare list are the two verbs of devotion that frame the names: lehuṁ nāmī ('bowing, I take up the name') and karuṁ ya sev ('I also render service'). Naming is itself an act of vandana — each name is uttered with a bow, not merely recited. The honorifics svāmī ('lord') and deva ('divine one') are woven between the names as titles, not additional figures. The small particle ya ('too, also') quietly stitches this verse's act of seva to the bowing of the line before, so the whole stanza reads as one unbroken gesture of homage rather than two separate sentences. It is worth noticing that Ṛṣabhānana ('bull-faced', echoing the first Tīrthaṅkara Ṛṣabha) also stands among the four śāśvata, ever-existing Jina-names — a hint that, in the hymn's reckoning, the living Mahāvideha Jinas and the eternal name-set quietly overlap.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The verse rests on a hallmark Jain conviction: an awakened guide is never wholly absent from the cosmos. Though Bharata-kṣetra has no living Tīrthaṅkara in the present fifth ara, twenty viharamāṇa Jinas are perpetually present and teaching across the five Mahāvideha realms (four in each). To name and serve them affirms that the tīrtha — the ford to liberation — is unbroken and ever-accessible, which is precisely how such recitation nourishes samyaktva (right faith). The act requested here also models the threefold bhakti of body, speech and mind: the bow (kāya), the spoken name (vacana) and the inward intent of sevā (mana) fuse into a single offering.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: As you chant these five names, let each be a genuine bow rather than a syllable rushed past. The verse trains the reciter to couple every holy name with a bodily and mental act of homage, so that the morning veneration becomes mindful and felt rather than mechanical.

crossReferences.text: Śrī Mandara-stavana — hymn to Sīmandhara on the twenty viharamāṇa Jinas
crossReferences.source: Ṛṣi Rāmakṛṣṇa; Tessitori Collection, Biblioteca Civica 'V. Joppi', Udine

crossReferences.text: Caturviṁśati-stava (Logassa) — the model of naming-as-veneration of the Jinas

sources.title: Viharamāna — definition (the twenty living Tīrthaṅkaras)
sources.consultedFor: Confirming viharamāṇa = the twenty currently-living Jinas of Mahāvideha, with Sīmandhara foremost.

sources.title: Simandhar Swami
sources.consultedFor: Mahāvideha cosmology: four Jinas in each of five Mahāvideha realms, totalling twenty living Tīrthaṅkaras.

sources.title: Ānana — definitions (Sanskrit 'face, mouth')
sources.consultedFor: Root of ānana in the name Ṛṣabhānana ('bull-faced').

sources.title: Vīrya — definitions (Sanskrit 'valour, energy')
sources.consultedFor: Root of vīrya in the name Anantavīrya ('of infinite valour').

notes: Context identifies this as Dhāla 4 (the viharamāṇa enumeration): the five names here are #5–9 of the twenty living Jinas, with #1–4 (Sīmandhar, Jugmandhar, Bāhu, Subāhu) in the preceding verse and #10–16 (Viśāla, Vajradhara, Candrānana…Nemiprabhu) in the following one. The Gujarati સુરપ્રભુ voices the standard name Sūraprabha (sūra 'sun' + prabhā) as sura-prabhu ('lord of the gods'), a devotional pun preserved verbatim. અનંતવીરજ carries a metrical -ja on Anantavīrya. Spellings (લેહું, કરું ય, સેવ) are kept as printed per the book's orthography. Online lists of the twenty names diverge in their later half; the surrounding verses of this hymn, not the variant web lists, fix the readings used here.
